questions.text: Which of the following are not polymer?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins are polymers of amino acids.
Polysaccharides are monomers of saccharide units (monosaccharides).
The structures of lipids vary widely with many containing long hydrocarbon tails or carbon ring structures, but no monomers.
Nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides .

questions.explanation: Lipids are fatty acid esters of alcohol and related substances. Polysaccharides are polymers of monosaccharides. Proteins are polymers of amino acids, and nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides.

questions.text: Which of the following is the least likely to be involved in stabilizing the three­ dimensional folding of most proteins?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hydrogen bonds stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.
Electrostatic interaction stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.
Hydrophobic interaction stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins and is a weak one.

Protein folding is the process by which a protein structure assumes its functional shape or conformation by allowing interaction between amino acids by various bond formations. All protein molecules are heterogeneous unbranched chains of amino acids. Different types of weak interactions hold the interacting segments together in protein. Hydrophobic interaction, H bonds, and electrostatic interactions are the weak ones and stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins, while ester bonds are covalent bonds and are least likely to be involved in stabilizing the three-dimensional folding of most proteins.

questions.explanation: Protein folding is the process by which a protein structure assumes its functional shape or conformation by allowing interaction between amino acids by various bond formations. All protein molecules are heterogeneous unbranched chains of amino acids. Different types of weak interactions hold the interacting segments together in protein. Hydrophobic interaction, H bonds, and electrostatic interactions are the weak ones and stabilize the tertiary structure of proteins, while ester bonds are covalent bonds and are least likely to be involved in stabilizing the three-dimensional folding of most proteins.

questions.text: The chitinous exoskeleton of arthropods is formed by the polymerisation of:

Chitin is the derivative of N-acetyl glucosamine monomers.
Chitin is the derivative of N-acetyl glucosamine monomers.

questions.text: Which one of the following biomolecules is correctly characterized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Lecithin a phosphorylated glyceride found in the cell membrane.
options.explanation: Lecithin is a triglyceride lipid where one fatty acid is replaced by phosphoric acid, which is linked to an additional nitrogenous group called choline. It is a common membrane lipid. It is an amphipathic phospholipid, having both hydrophilic polar and hydrophobic nonpolar groups. The hydrocarbon chains of two fatty acids function as hydrophobic non­polar tails, whereas the phosphate and choline behave as a hydrophilic polar head group of the molecule.

options.text: Palmitic acid ­an unsaturated fatty acid with 18 carbon atoms.
options.explanation: Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid (as it does not possess double bonds in its carbon chain) and contains 16 carbon atoms with formula C 16 H 32 O2.

options.text: Adenylic acid adenosine with a glucose phosphate molecule.
options.explanation: Adenylic acid or adenosine monophosphate is a nucleotide formed by the union of adenine (nitrogenous base), ribose (pentose sugar), and phosphate. It is formed through phosphorylation of nucleoside as phosphate combines with sugar molecule at its 5 carbon atom.

options.text: Alanine amino acid­ contains an amino group and an acidic group anywhere in the molecule.
options.explanation: Amino acids are organic acids with a carboxylic group (–COOH) having an amino group (– NH 2) generally attached to a­ carbon or carbon next to a carboxylic group. Alanine is a nonpolar and neutral amino acid having one methyl group and having an amino group attached to the carbon next to the carboxylic group.

questions.explanation: Lecithin is a triglyceride lipid where one fatty acid is replaced by phosphoric acid, which is linked to an additional nitrogenous group called choline. It is a common membrane lipid. It is an amphipathic phospholipid, having both hydrophilic polar and hydrophobic nonpolar groups. The hydrocarbon chains of two fatty acids function as hydrophobic non­polar tails, whereas the phosphate and choline behave as a hydrophilic polar head group of the molecule. Palmitic acid is a saturated fatty acid (as it does not possess double bonds in its carbon chain) and contains 16 carbon atoms with formula C 16 H 32 O2. Adenylic acid or adenosine monophosphate is a nucleotide formed by the union of adenine (nitrogenous base), ribose (pentose sugar), and phosphate. It is formed through phosphorylation of nucleoside as phosphate combines with sugar molecule at its 5 carbon atom. Amino acids are organic acids with a carboxylic group (–COOH) having an amino group (– NH 2) generally attached to a­ carbon or carbon next to a carboxylic group. Alanine is a nonpolar and neutral amino acid having one methyl group and having an amino group attached to the carbon next to the carboxylic group.

questions.text: Carbohydrates are commonly found as starch in plants storage organs. Which of the following five properties of starch make it useful as a storage material? (1) Easily translocated (2) Chemically non­reactive (3) Easily digested by animals (4) Osmotically inactive (5) Synthesized during photosynthesis The useful properties are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 1,3 and 5 properties donot tell about its storage function.
1 and 5 properties are irrelevant to its storage function.
3 property doenot tell anything about its storage function.

Starch is the major storage carbohydrate of plants. In most plant species it is accumulated in the chloroplast of leaves, whereas in storage organs it accumulates in the amyloplast as reserve starch. It is the osmotically inactive form of photosynthetic product and is a hexosan polysaccharide made of many glucose units, so it is chemically non-reactive.

questions.explanation: Starch is the major storage carbohydrate of plants. In most plant species it is accumulated in the chloroplast of leaves, whereas in storage organs it accumulates in the amyloplast as reserve starch. It is the osmotically inactive form of photosynthetic product and is a hexosan polysaccharide made of a large number of glucose units so it is chemically non-reactive.

questions.text: The major role of minor elements inside living organisms is to act as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Minor elements are those which are required in the quantity of less than milligram/gram of dry matter, but they are essential for proper growth and development of an organism e.g., Cl, Mn, B, Zn, Cu, Mo, etc. These elements work as non­protein cofactor in enzymes e.g., Zn, Cu, etc. They also take part in oxidation-reduction reactions, e.g., Cu, with variable valency. Chloride ion enhances the activity of salivary amylase. Zinc is required for the activity of carbonic anhydrase and alcohol dehydrogenase, etc.

options.text: Building blocks of important amino acids
options.explanation: Minor elements do not form building blocks of amino acids.
Hormones are glycoproteins, having sugar attached with proteins. Therefore, minor elements are not constituents of hormones.
Minor elements are not binder of cell structure.

questions.explanation: Minor elements are those which are required in the quantity of less than milligram/gram of dry matter, but they are essential for proper growth and development of an organism e.g., Cl, Mn, B, Zn, Cu, Mo, etc. These elements work as non­protein cofactor in enzymes e.g., Zn, Cu, etc. They also take part in oxidation-reduction reactions, e.g., Cu, with variable valency. Chloride ion enhances the activity of salivary amylase. Zinc is required for the activity of carbonic anhydrase and alcohol dehydrogenase, etc.

questions.text: Which of the following is a reducing sugar?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: All monosaccharides are reducing sugars.
These are not pure carbohydrates. Pure carbohydrates like monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides are classified as reducing and non-reducing sugar.
These are not pure carbohydrates. Pure carbohydrates like monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, and polysaccharides are classified as reducing and non-reducing sugar.
Sucrose is non-reducing sugar.

questions.explanation: All those sugars which have free aldehyde or ketone group are called reducing sugars. These are able to reduce cupric ions (Cu+2) into cuprous ions (Cu+). Sucrose and starch are non-­reducing sugars.

questions.text: Which of the following have carbohydrates as a prosthetic group?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glycoproteins are proteins that contain sugars like carbohydrates as a prosthetic group. In most glycoproteins, the linkage is between asparagine and N­acetyl­D­glucosamine. Some glycoproteins are immunoglobulins, membrane proteins, and muscle proteins.
Chromoproteins are proteins associated with pigments, e.g., cytochrome, phytochrome.
Lipoproteins are protein complexed with lipids like triglycerides, phospholipids, etc
Nucleoproteins are proteins associated with nucleic acids.

questions.explanation: Glycoproteins are proteins that contain sugars like carbohydrates as a prosthetic group. In most glycoproteins, the linkage is between asparagine and N­-acetyl­ D-­glucosamine. Some glycoproteins are immunoglobulins, membrane proteins, and muscle proteins. Lipoproteins are protein complexed with lipids like triglycerides, phospholipids, etc. Nucleoproteins are proteins associated with nucleic acids and chromoproteins are proteins associated with pigments e.g., cytochrome, phytochrome.

questions.text: How many water molecules will be released upon the formation of a trisaccharide?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.

The formation of a trisaccharide involves the linking of three monosaccharide molecules (simple sugars) through glycosidic bonds. In the process of forming a glycosidic bond, a water molecule (H2O) is released as a byproduct. The general reaction for the formation of a trisaccharide can be represented as follows: Monosaccharide 1 + Monosaccharide 2 + Monosaccharide 3 → Trisaccharide + 3H2O. As we can see from the reaction, for each glycosidic bond formed between two monosaccharides, one water molecule is released. Since a trisaccharide involves the formation of two glycosidic bonds, two water molecules are released in the process. Therefore, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released.
Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.
Since, upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Upon the formation of a trisaccharide, two water molecules will be released.

questions.text: Which of the following is an amino acid and part of phospholipids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Serine is an amino acid and is indeed a component of phospholipids. It plays a crucial role in the structure of phosphatidylserine, a type of phospholipid found in cell membranes.

Choline is not an amino acid, but it is a crucial component of phospholipids. It's a quaternary ammonium salt containing a hydrophilic head, making it important for the structure of phosphatidylcholine, a common phospholipid in cell membranes.
Ethanolamine is not an amino acid, but like choline, it is a vital part of phospholipids. It contributes to the structure of phosphatidylethanolamine, another phospholipid in cell membranes.
Aspartic acid is an amino acid, but is not typically found as a component of phospholipids.

questions.explanation: Serine and aspartic acids are examples of amino acids, while choline and ethanolamine are Nz-containing bases. Serine can be a constituent of phospholipids and can act as a nitrogenous base.

questions.text: What is the myelin sheath of a neuron composed of?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sphingolipids, including sphingomyelin, are a class of lipids that make up a significant part of the myelin sheath. They provide structural support and insulation for nerve fibers.
Choline is not a major component of the myelin sheath. While it is important for various cellular functions, it is not a primary structural component of myelin.
Ethanolamine is not a primary component of the myelin sheath either. Like choline, it has other roles in cell membranes but is not a key structural element of myelin.
Waxes are not typically found in the myelin sheath of neurons. Myelin is primarily a lipid-rich substance composed of sphingolipids and proteins.

questions.explanation: The myelin sheath of neurons is composed of sphingolipids. Ethanolamine and choline are nitrogenous bases found in phospholipids while axes are abundantly found in protective covering of fruits and leaves.

questions.text: A protein having secondary structure possesses:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Yes, proteins with secondary structures often involve hydrogen bonding. In secondary structures like alpha-helices and beta-sheets, hydrogen bonds form between the carbonyl oxygen of one amino acid and the amide hydrogen of another along the polypeptide chain. These hydrogen bonds help stabilize the secondary structure.

Disulfide bonds are covalent bonds formed between the sulfur atoms of two cysteine amino acids. While disulfide bonds play a role in stabilizing the tertiary structure of proteins, they are not typically associated with secondary structures.
Peptide bonds are covalent bonds that link amino acids together in a polypeptide chain. They are present in all proteins, both in primary and secondary structures.

The correct answer is “Both A and C.” Proteins with secondary structures do possess hydrogen bonding (Option A) as a key stabilizing factor, and they also have peptide bonds (Option C) as these bonds link the amino acids in the polypeptide chain.

questions.explanation: All proteins have primary structure; amino acids are joined with each other through peptide bonds. In Secondary structure, polypeptide chains coil to form either a helixes or B pleated sheets or both through hydrogen bonds.
